cards.text: The Philippines still claims Sabah. Malaysia has won every court case. The underlying question has never been answered by any court.
cards.sub: In February 2026, the Philippines' national mapping agency published a map showing Sabah as Philippine territory. The DFA reaffirmed sovereignty. Malaysia protested. This has happened before — in various forms, for 148 years. Malaysia won a $14.9 billion arbitration annulment in Paris in December 2025, a Dutch Supreme Court ruling in 2024, and a French cassation in 2024. But no court has ever ruled on whether the 1878 agreement was a lease or a cession — because Malaysia has never consented to the ICJ hearing it.

cards.h: One word in one 1878 document — 'ceded' or 'leased' — has generated 148 years of dispute. No court of competent jurisdiction has ever ruled on which translation is correct.
cards.s: On 22 January 1878, the Sultan of Sulu signed an agreement with British merchants transferring North Borneo. The English text says 'ceded' — permanent. The Malay/Sulu text uses 'pajakkan' — which the Philippines translates as 'leased.' Malaysia's position: cession, full stop. The Philippines' position: lease, still running. Every court case Malaysia has won — The Hague (2023), Netherlands Supreme Court (2024), Paris (2025) — was about arbitration procedure, not the merits of this question. The ICJ could rule on the merits — but only with Malaysia's consent, which Malaysia has never given.
cards.d: Sources: 1878 agreement text, RSIS analysis, UP Law Faculty paper, ICJ Statute Article 36, Malaysia Sulu Case portal. The Paris Court of Appeal (Dec 2025) annulled the $14.9B award specifically because the arbitration clause in the 1878 agreement was 'inapplicable' — the reference to the British Consul General as arbitrator had 'disappeared.' This is a procedural finding, not a ruling on sovereignty.

cards.h: This is not an abstract legal dispute. In 2013, armed followers of a Sulu claimant invaded eastern Sabah. 68 people died. The Philippines' claim has manifested as armed incursion within living memory.
cards.s: In February 2013, approximately 200 armed followers of self-proclaimed Sultan Jamalul Kiram III landed in Lahad Datu, eastern Sabah. Malaysian security forces launched a military operation. 68 people were killed — 56 militants, 6 civilians, 6 Malaysian security personnel. The ESSZONE (Eastern Sabah Security Zone) and ESSCOM (Security Command) were created in response. After Lahad Datu, Malaysia stopped paying the annual RM5,300 'cession money' to Sulu heirs — a payment it had maintained since 1963. The cross-border corridor between the Sulu archipelago and eastern Sabah remains porous.
cards.d: Sources: Malaysian security records, Lahad Datu incident documentation. The Philippines distanced itself from the incursion but never formally renounced the underlying claim that motivated it.

cards.h: Sabah unanimously rejects the Philippine claim. But Sabah also has unresolved grievances with Kuala Lumpur that the external threat conveniently obscures.
cards.s: Every major Sabah party — across government and opposition — has condemned the Philippine claim. Sabah PKR slammed the fresh calls in March 2026. But Sabah's relationship with the federal government is not without tension: the special grant under Article 112C of the Federal Constitution has been frozen at RM26.7 million since 1969 despite a constitutional requirement for periodic review. Revenue-sharing disputes, development gaps, and unfulfilled MA63 promises persist. The external sovereignty dispute makes it harder, not easier, for Sabah to negotiate its internal position — because raising Sabah grievances during a sovereignty crisis can be framed as disloyalty.
cards.d: Sources: FMT (Sabah PKR March 2026), Jesselton Times, MA63 documentation. T4A has previously published Issue 1719 on the frozen special grant.

cards.h: The question is not whether Malaysia owns Sabah. International law, self-determination, and 63 years of statehood answer that. The question is why, after winning every court case, Malaysia still won't let the ICJ rule on the merits — and why the Philippines can't drop a claim enshrined in a 1968 law no president dares repeal.
cards.text: Malaysia's legal position is strong. But strength and finality are different things. A country that has won in The Hague, Paris, and Amsterdam — and still refuses ICJ jurisdiction — is a country that has won on procedure while leaving the underlying argument permanently available to anyone who wants to revive it.

cards.h: Malaysia wins on law. The Philippines loses on law. Neither has resolved the dispute. And the people who live in the space between — the Sulu-origin communities of eastern Sabah, the security forces of ESSZONE, the state government negotiating with KL — bear the cost of a question two nations refuse to close.
cards.text: The Philippines cannot drop the Sabah claim because RA5446 is law and no president will repeal it. Malaysia will not submit to the ICJ because the risk — however small — of an adverse ruling on a core territory is existential. So the dispute continues as a cycle: periodic diplomatic flare-ups, maps, protests, and arbitration attempts that collapse on procedure. Meanwhile, Sabah's own priorities — the frozen special grant, development equity, MA63 fulfilment — get less airtime every time Manila publishes a new map.

sources: ICJ filings, Paris Court of Appeal (Dec 2025), Hague Court of Appeal (2023), Netherlands Supreme Court (2024), DFA Philippines (Feb 2026), Malaysian FM statement, RSIS, UP Law Faculty, FMT, Jesselton Times, Business Mirror, SCMP, Malay Mail, Bloomberg, Global Arbitration Review, Malaysia Sulu Case portal, Lowy Institute
confidence: High on court rulings (all documented and published). High on historical facts (1878 agreement, MA63, Lahad Datu). High on recent developments (NAMRIA map, DFA statement Feb 2026). Medium on Sabah internal politics (sentiment is clear but detailed polling unavailable).
